Appendix 14 C(α,γ) 18

div.head: O
div.p: We follow the analysis of [113] that includes four contributions to the 14 C(α,γ) 18 O reaction rate: resonant capture via the subthreshold state at E x = 6.198 MeV (J π = 1 -) through the high energy tail and two resonant states at E x = 6.404 MeV (J π = 3 -) and 7.12 MeV (J π = 4 + ) in 18 O, respectively, as well as the direct capture. [204] measured the resonance strength for the 7.12 MeV state directly and provided a low energy Sfactor extrapolation for the direct capture component, based on measurements above 1.14 MeV. [113] used the ( 6 Li,d) and ( 7 Li,t) α-transfer reactions in inverse kinematics at Florida State University to determine the α-width of the resonance state at 6.404 MeV and the ANC of the subthreshold resonance state at 6.198 MeV. The experimentally unknown sign of the interference between the predominantly p-wave direct capture and the subthreshold resonance introduces a several orders of magnitude uncertainty at very low energies relevant for temperatures below 0.03 GK, which is not relevant for X-ray Appendix 17 O(p,α) 14

div.head: N
div.p: For T ≤ 1.0 GK, the 17 O(p,α) 14 N reaction rate reported by [139] is adopted in the present evaluation. The rate is dominated by four resonances and mostly based on the detailed evaluation of [212] but includes an updated resonance strength for the 65 keV resonance from a direct measurement at the LUNA accelerator at the Laboratori Nazionali del Gran Sasso (LNGS) of INFN, Italy underground laboratory that increases the rate at temperatures below 0.1 GK by a factor of 2. Above T = 1.0 GK we use the slope predicted by the NON-SMOKER Hauser-Feshbach model [213] and normalize it to the rate at 1 GK. The reaction rate is listed in Table 2.
Appendix 17 F(p,γ) 18

div.head: Ne
div.p: The reaction rate at X-ray burst temperatures is dominated by a 3 + resonance at 599.8 keV, for which the resonance strength has been measured directly using a 17 F radioactive beam at the ORNL DRS recoil separator [102], albeit with a ≈100% error bar. At low temperatures direct capture is expected to dominate. The relevant spectroscopic factors have been determined from 17 F(d,n) 18 Ne proton transfer reaction measurements in inverse kinematics at Florida State University [74], who extracted asymptotic normalization coefficients (ANC) of the bound states in 18 Ne. We adopt the new direct capture rate and the compiled resonance parameters from [74] for T ≤ 0.9 GK. For T > 0.9 GK, the NON-SMOKER Hauser-Feshbach reaction rate [213], normalized to the adopted rate at T = 0.9 GK, is used. The numerical results are listed in Table 3.

Appendix 18 F(p,α) 15 O R-matrix calculations of the cross section are based on a mix of relatively sparse and uncertain direct cross section measurements, and indirect studies of various excited states around the proton threshold in 19 Ne that provide resonant contributions. Possible unknown interference between resonances as well as incomplete level information results in significant uncertainties (see the most recent analysis of [215]). Since the evaluation of [207] previously recommended in JINA REACLIB, a number of indirect studies provided additional information on excited states: 18 F(d,n) measurements at ORNL identified a subthreshold resonance [107], for which spin and parity 1/2 + are later determined using the 20 Ne(p,d) 19 Ne reaction with the JENSA gas jet target at ORNL [126,214]. In addition several 19 F( 3 He,t) 19 Ne measurements at Garching and Argonne National Laboratory provided additional level information [135,136], including improved constraints on low lying 3/2 + resonances [134,215]. Cross section data were also obtained using the Trojan horse method via the three-body reaction 2 H( 18 F,α 15 O)n [119,216]. R-matrix calculations including the Trojan horse data [216] found results consistent with the Rmatrix analysis of [214]. The latest analysis [215] differs from [214] mainly in a greatly reduced uncertainty of the reaction rate below 0.3 GK, and provides an upper and a lower reaction rate based on different resonance interference assumptions. We use here an average rate. In the 0.1-0.4 GK temperature range, this rate is up to 60% larger than the previously recommended rate of [207], while for higher temperature the rates agree and we continue to to use [207] for temperatures up to 10 GK. The numerical results of 18 F(p,α) 15 O reaction rate are listed in Table 3.

Appendix 18 F(p,γ) 19 Ne Above 0.2 GK the rate is thought to be dominated by a 330 keV resonance [207]. [103] performed a direct measurement of the resonance strength of the 665 keV resonance, potentially contributing to the rate below 0.2 GK, using the DRAGON recoil separator at TRIUMF and demonstrated, albeit with only two counts, that the resonance strength is significantly lower than previous upper limits making its contribution to the rate negligible. We adopt the analysis of [103], who for the other contributions to the low temperature rate employed the resonance parameters of [126,214], creating consistency with our recommended 18 F(p,α) reaction rate. The new rate is significantly lower below 0.2 GK compared to the previously recommended Monte Carlo rate from [207] due to the lower contribution of the 665 keV resonance that was previously treated using an upper limit. For example, at 0.15 GK the new rate is a factor of 4 lower than the lower limit provided by [207]. For T > 0.5 GK, the reaction rate is dominated by the resonance at E cm = 330 keV [217].The final 18 F(p,γ) 19 Ne reaction rates are obtained from combining the rates in these two temperature ranges, and are listed in Table 3.

For the 19 F(p,α) 16 O reaction, transitions to 16 O in the ground state (p,α 0 ), the excited state at E x = 6.05 MeV (pair emitting, p,α π ), and the sum of transitions to all other γ-emitting excited states (p,α γ ) are considered separately.
Appendix 19 Ne(p,γ) 20

div.head: Na
div.p: Two reaction channels, capture on the ground state 19 Ne g.s. (p,γ) 20 Na and capture on the first excited 0.238 MeV target state 19 Ne e.x. (p,γ) 20 Na are considered. Capture on the third excited 19 Ne state at 0.275 MeV is negligible.

For 19 Ne g.s. (p,γ) 20 Na, four resonances at E r = 0.456, 0.660, 0.797, and 0.887 MeV, as well as the direct capture contribution, determine the reaction rate. For E r = 0.456 MeV we adopt the resonance strength from the direct measurement with the DRAGON recoil separator at TRIUMF [104]. The spin of the E r = 0.661 MeV resonance is not certain. ENSDF currently assigns 3 + to the corresponding E x = 2.849 MeV state based on (p,n) and two ( 3 He,t) measurements. This is in line with earlier reaction rate evaluations [222,223]. However, a more recent (d,n) experiment seems to indicate a preference for a 1 + assignment [108]. More experimental data would be desirable to clarify the nature of this state. We adopt here the 3 + assignment and the resonance strength of [223]. Using the 1 + assignment and the larger resonance strength of [108] (21 meV) has only a modest impact, resulting in a reaction rate that is identical up to around 1 GK and is then lower by up to 30% for higher temperatures (see Figure 22). For the higher lying resonances as well as for direct capture we use the parameters from [223].

For 19 Ne e.x. (p,γ) 20 Na only the resonances at E r = 0.218 and 0.422 MeV, corresponding to the same states as the ground state resonances at E r = 0.456 and 0.660 MeV, respectively, contribute significantly. For E r = 0.218 MeV we adopt the resonance strength of [108], who derived it from their measurement of the proton branchings via a (d,n) transfer reaction at the RESOLUT facility at Florida State University in combination with the direct resonance strength measurement for the ground state rate from [104]. For the E r = 0.218 MeV resonance we follow [223]. The total reaction rate of 19 Ne(p,γ) 20 Na is listed in Table 4.

A recent active target direct measurement in inverse kinematics using the MUSIC detector at the ATLAS facility at Argonne National Laboratory [144] resolved discrepancies among previous measurements and confirmed the excellent agreement of experimental cross section data with statistical model calculations using TALYS with the Mc-Fadden/Satchler α-nucleus potential [224]. The experiment measured the angle and final excitation energy integrated cross section. We calculated the 23 Na(α,p) 26 Mg reaction rate from the statistical model cross section displayed in [144] and apply a SEF. The results are listed in Table 4.
Appendix 23 Na(p,γ) 24

div.head: Mg
div.p: The rate below 1 GK is dominated by direct capture and three resonances. For the three resonances we adopt the updated strengths from [140], who performed direct measurements at the LUNA underground accelerator. They reported the first direct measurement of the resonance at E CM r =135.4 keV (their E Lab r =140 keV) ( [225] reported their tentative detection at the LENA facility as an upper limit), a more accurate resonance strength for the E CM r =242.2 keV resonance (their E Lab r =251 keV) and a confirmation of the previously measured resonance strengths of the E CM r =298.1 keV resonance (their E Lab r =309 keV). For the first resonance, we adopt the updated resonance energy E CM r =133(3) keV from a measurement using the ( 3 He,d) transfer reaction at the Enge split-pole spectrograph at TUNL [146], which agrees within uncertainties with the energy reported in [140] but is 5 keV lower than what was adopted in some earlier compilations (e.g. [225]) based on older measurements (see the overview in [140]). For higher lying resonances that start playing a significant role above 1 GK we adopt the properties in [207]. For direct capture we use the updated S-factors from [226] that are based on direct measurements at the University of Notre Dame and an R-matrix analysis. While we For T ≤ 1.25 GK, we follow the updated resonance analysis of [227], who re-evaluate experimental data from various direct measurements with data from inelastic scattering of alphas, protons, and electrons on 28 Si performed at iThemba LABS in South Africa. In the relevant temperature range for X-ray bursts (T > 0.5 GK) the new rate is largely identical to the STARLIB [207] reaction rate, though there are some deviations at lower temperatures that may impact other applications. Above T = 1.25 GK, the STARLIB reaction rate is used. The numerical results are given in Table 5.

Appendix 25 Mg(p,γ) 26 Al 26 Al has a T 1/2 =717,000 yr ground state, and a T 1/2 =6.346 s excited isomeric state. The ground and isomeric state are thought to be in thermal equilibrium for T > 0.4 GK [229] and our X-ray burst model therefore includes only a fully thermalized 26 Al. However, for other applications it is critical to track the ground and isomeric state separately. JINA REACLIB therefore includes separate 25 Mg(p,γ) 26 Al g.s and 25 Mg(p,γ) 26 Al isomer rates that are added to obtain the total 25 Mg(p,γ) 26 Al rate feeding a thermalized 26 Al. The rates are calculated using the strengths of individual narrow resonances, and the factor f 0 denoting the feeding fraction to the 26 Al ground state. At X-ray burst temperatures the resonances at 304 keV and above dominate, while for other astrophysical sites lower lying resonances are important. We largely use the resonance parameters from STARLIB [207] with the following updates: for the 92.2 keV, 189.5 keV and 304 keV resonances we use new resonance strengths from direct measurements at the LUNA underground accelerator [142,141] following the evaluation of [228]. We also use the corresponding updated f 0 values, except for the 92.2 keV resonance, where we adopt the more precise value obtained from a recent γ-spectroscopy experiment at Argonne National Laboratory using a fusion evaporation reaction with the Gammasphere detector array [137]. For the 57.7 keV resonance, a recent indirect measurement using the 25 Mg( 7 Li, 6 He) 26 Al transfer reaction at the HI-13 tandem accelerator in Beijing [147] claimed a more precise resonance strength of 2.93 ± 0.50 × 10 -13 . The central value is almost identical with the STARLIB adopted value of 2.8 ± 1.1 × 10 -13 , nevertheless we adopt the value from [147]. The SEF is considered for both channels. The evaluated reaction rates of 25 Mg(p,γ) 26 Al g.s and 25 Mg(p,γ) 26 Al isomer are presented in Table 5.
Appendix 23 Al(p,γ) 24

div.head: Si
div.p: The reaction rate at X-ray burst temperatures is dominated by a single resonance corresponding to the second 2 + state in 24 Si. [231] determined an excitation energy of E x =3441(10) keV using the 28 Si( 4 He, 8 He) transfer reaction. This places the resonance at a relatively low energy that is just high enough to dominate the rate, resulting in an [129] performed in-beam γ-spectroscopy of 25 Si via a 1n knockout reaction at NSCL at Michigan State University and identified 2 states above the proton threshold, a 9/2 + state at 3695 (14) keV and a 1/2 + state at 3802 (11) keV. This result confirmed older previous conference proceedings reports of a possible resonance at 3.7 MeV identified in ( 3 He, 6 He) transfer reaction studies [232]. We adopt the resonance parameters in [129] obtained with a Q-value of 3.414 MeV, who complement their excitation energy measurements with shell model calculations for resonance widths and additional excited states. The reaction rate is found to be dominated by the 9/2 + resonance. The numerical reaction rates are given in Table 6.
Appendix 25 Al(p,γ) 26

div.head: Si
div.p: We follow largely [167] in considering 4 resonances to contribute to the 25 Al(p,γ) 26 Si rate. The rate for X-ray burst conditions is largely dominated by a single 3 + resonance, for which we determine a resonance energy of 415.4(8) keV from the ENSDF recommended 26 Si excitation energy [233] and the AME20 mass table [157]. This resonance energy is in agreement with but more precise than the directly determined resonance energy from the detected proton energy in 26 P β-delayed proton emission measurements at Lanzhou (418(8) keV) [167] and Michigan State University (412(2) keV) [125]. The resonance strength can be determined from the measured proton width by combining (d,n) proton transfer reaction measurements in inverse kinematics at Florida State University [109] and the ratio of proton to γ-width obtained from the proton and γ-intensity ratio of the 5929 keV state in 26 Si measured by β-delayed proton emission. For the proton intensity we adopt the value from [167], who confirm the lower proton intensity measured earlier by [124], which is significantly lower than the value reported by [123] from a measurement in GANIL. For the γ-intensity we use the result from [125] measured at NSCL at Michigan State University. The resulting resonance strength is 32 meV, which is significantly larger than the values reported in the review of [234] owing to the lower proton branching. For the other three resonances we adopt the shell model calculated resonance strengths from [167]. Their contribution is mostly negligible, with the 432 keV 4 + resonance contributing up to 10% at the highest relevant temperature of 2 GK. The numerical reaction rates are given in Table 6.
Appendix 26 Al(p,γ) 27

div.head: Si
div.p: The ground and isomeric state reaction rates of 26 Al g.s. (p,γ) 27 Si and 26 Al m (p,γ) 27 Si are respectively evaluated. These two sets of rates are used to compute the total thermalized 26 Al(p,γ) 27 Si reaction rate, considering the thermal populations of the ground state and isomeric state of 26 Al (Eq. ( 4) in Ref. [236]).

For the 26 Al g.s. (p,γ) 27 Si rate, [114,239] recently performed (d,p) neutron transfer measurements on 26 Al g.s. using a 6 MeV/A 26 Al beam at TRIUMF detecting reaction protons with the TUDA array, in order to determine spectroscopic factors for resonances in the 27 Al isospin mirror. They extracted spectroscopic factors using a global Koning-Delaroche optical model parameterization [171] for three resonances at E r =127, 189, and 276 keV. For the 127 and 276 keV resonances we adopt their resulting resonance strengths that also show excellent agreement with shell model calculations. For the 189 keV resonance, there are, in addition two direct measurements of the resonance strength available. [235] used a proton beam impinging on a 26 Al radioactive target and obtained ωγ =55(9) µeV, however the result was not published in a refereed journal. More recently, [237] obtained a significantly lower ωγ =33(7) µeV using a 26 Al radioactive beam and the DRAGON recoil separator at TRIUMF. The indirect (d,p) measurement from [114] reports an even slightly lower ωγ =26(7) µeV. Given that [237] and [114] agree within error bars and point to a significantly lower resonance strength than [235], we chose a weighted average of [237] and [114] only. However, to account for possible underestimated systematic errors in the indirect approach, for example from the use of a global optical potential, and the assumption of mirror symmetry, we doubled their reported error of 25%, resulting in a weighted average of ωγ =33 µeV. For all other resonances we use the resonance strengths recommended in [238]. For the resonances at 68 keV and 369 keV these strengths are compatible with the upper limits from [114]. Figure 23 shows the ratio of the present calculation to the compiled Monte Carlo reaction rate [238]. The differences between the two rates are caused by introducing the new resonance parameters of [114] in the present evaluation.

For 26 Al m (p,γ) 27 Si we take into account the resonances and upper limits for resonance strengths in Table II in [151]. These limits were obtained using a 26 Si(d,p) 27 Si neutron transfer reaction at NSCL at Michigan State University, taking advantage of the fact that the 26 Si ground state is part of the same isospin triplet as 26 Al isomer . These limits are compatible with, but much more stringent than the limits obtained from 26 Al m (d,p) 27 measurements with a 26 Al isomer beam at Argonne National Laboratory [149]. The rate obtained with these upper limits is then compared with the NACRE evaluation for each temperature, and the smaller value is chosen as the recommended rate for 26 Al m (p,γ) 27 Si.

The total thermalized 26 Al(p,γ) 27 Si rate is finally calculated by adding the rates for 26 Al g.s. (p,γ) 27 Si and 26 Al m (p,γ) 27 Si weighted with the Boltzmann population of the respective levels as a function of temperature. The numerical results are given in Table 7.
Appendix 26 Si(p,γ) 27

div.head: P
div.p: The reaction rate is dominated by a single resonance at E r =318 keV corresponding to the first excited 3/2 + state at 1125 keV in 27 P. The resonance energy had recently been revised upwards by 64 keV based on measurements of the proton energies in a 27 S β-delayed proton γ spectroscopy experiment at Lanzhou [240]. Combining this result with the well known 26 Si mass and the known excitation energy of the first excited state in 27 P from γ-ray spectroscopy leads to a revised 27 P mass, that has been adopted in AME20 and deviates significantly from previous estimates, including estimates using isobaric multiplets. The new resonance energy agrees well with the Coulomb dissociation experiment performed at RIKEN [122] (315(17) keV). However, it disagrees with the Coulomb dissociation experiment of [121] at R3B, GSI (267(20) keV) which would be more in line with the previous estimate. We adopt in this work the resonance strength of ωγ =2.9 meV from [240], who derive it using their measured γ-to-proton branching ratio, and the γ-width estimated by [207]. This resonance strength is about a factor of 2 smaller than the strength derived based on a recent (d,p) transfer reaction measurement at the Czech Academy of Science [241]. In that experiment, ANC and proton width of the isospin mirror are extracted from data, and combined with the same γ-to-proton branching ratio from [240], resulting in a correspondingly larger γ-width. However, the here adopted resonance strength is in reasonable agreement with previous Coulomb Dissociation experiments that obtain ωγ = 0.7 meV [121] and ωγ = 2 meV [122]. Direct capture starts to dominate at temperatures below 0.1 GK, where the rate is too slow to matter in X-ray bursts. Nevertheless, we include the direct capture component obtained from [241]. For completeness we include two higher lying resonances corresponding to the second and third excited 5/2 + states in 27 P. The resonance energies of E r = 762 keV and 1054 keV have been measured using β-delayed protons [240]. For the resonance strengths, we adopt the average of all previous measurements provided by [240] ωγ =0.65 meV, and an average of the Coulomb Dissociation experiments [121,122] ωγ =1 meV, respectively. The 26 Si(p,γ) 27 P reaction rates corrected by the SEF are given in Table 8.
Appendix 29 Si(p,γ) 30

div.head: P
div.p: The reaction rate is derived from 13 resonances below E r = 500 keV compiled in [242] who updated with new data from a γ-spectroscopy experiment, as well as 73 resonances above E r = 500 keV using the resonance properties from [207]. The calculated reaction rates, corrected with an SEF, are given in Table 8.

The rate provided in [243] is adopted here, which is largely based on their earlier compilation [130] that summarizes previous work and new information on 30 S levels from 28 Si( 3 He,n) and 32 S(p,t) transfer reaction measurements performed at the University of Notre Dame and RCNP at Osaka University, respectively. [243] does include an additional resonance at E r = 820 keV (3 + state) identified in a ( 3 He,n) γ-ray spectroscopy experiment at Argonne National Laboratory using the Gammasphere detector array [132] and uses an updated Q-value. [132] confirmed spins and assignments of the important 30 S states to states in the 30 Si isospin mirror, the latter being used to obtain the proton spectroscopic factors. Based on the current information on 25 states above the proton threshold in 30 S, the rate at X-ray burst temperatures is dominated by a E r = 410 keV 2 + resonance, with a few additional resonances coming into play above around 1.5 GK. For temperatures below 0.3 GK the E r = 289 keV 3 + resonance becomes dominant. An SEF is applied and the results are given in Table 8.

Ten resonances are considered for this reaction rate. We follow largely the compilation of [110], with updates from [244], and information for additional states from shell model calculations [60]. For the resonances at E r =196 keV and 261.9 keV we use the resonance strengths derived from (d,nγ) transfer reaction measurements at NSCL at Michigan State University [110]. The same experiment also provided upper limits for the resonance strengths of the E r =226.7 keV and 411.3 keV resonances, which we adopt as values. We note that for the important 411.3 keV resonance, shell model calculations indicate a resonance strength about a factor of 30 above the experimental upper limit. For the important E r =259.6 keV resonance we use the resonance strengths obtained from β-delayed proton measurements [244]. For the other resonances at E r =116 keV, Appendix 33 Cl(p,γ) 34

div.head: Ar
div.p: The 33 Cl(p,γ) 34 Ar reaction rate is calculated from the updated resonance parameters given in [138], who performed γ-spectroscopy of 34 Ar populated by a fusion evaporation reaction at Argonne National Laboratory using the GRETINA detector array. The measurement results in more precise excitation energies for 4 previously known states above the proton threshold, as well as the discovery of a fifth resonance at a resonance energy of 397 keV corresponding to a new state in 34 Ar at 5060.8 keV. [138] determined proton spectroscopic factors and γ-widths for these 5 resonances from mirror nucleus information, complemented with shell model calculations. The new 397 keV resonance is found to dominate the reaction rate above 0.2 GK. The previously recommended NON-SMOKER Hauser-Feshbach rate [213] agrees well with the new resonant rate at 0.8 GK and is used for temperatures above 0.8 GK to account for additional higher lying resonances. The calculated reaction rates are given in Table 9.

Appendix 34 Cl(p,γ) 35 Ar 34 Cl has a low lying excited state at 146 keV, which is a long-lived isomer under terrestrial conditions with a half-life of 32 min. The reaction rates on ground ( 34 Cl g.s. (p,γ) 35 Ar) and isomeric ( 34 Cl m (p,γ) 35 Ar) states are therefore evaluated separately, and in addition, a thermalized 34 Cl(p,γ) 35 Ar reaction rate is calculated by adding the 34 Cl g.s. (p,γ) 35 Ar and 34 Cl m (p,γ) 35 Ar rates weighted by their respective thermal population (Eq. ( 4) in [236]). According to calculation TALYS, the contributions from the higher lying thermally populated excited states in 34 Cl are negligible.

As experimental information on the relevant states in 35 Ar remains too sparse for a reaction rate calculation, we adopt the shell model calculations from [246]. They compare calculations with spectroscopic factors of states in the 35 Cl mirror nucleus obtained from ( 3 He,d) transfer reaction measurements at Garching [245] and (α,p) reaction studies at the TUNL Enge split-pole spectrometer [133], as well as information on excited states in 35 Ar from 36 Ar(d,t) 35 Ar reaction measurements at Garching [131] and find the impact of differences on the final rate to be within the quoted rate uncertainties obtained from a Monte Carlo approach. We calculate the 34 Cl g.s. (p,γ) 35 Ar reaction rate with the parameters of the resonant states listed in Table II in [246], and the 34 Cl m (p,γ) 35 Ar reaction rate with the parameters of the resonant states listed in Table III in [246].

Note that the highest energy of the listed resonances is E r = 1.16 MeV, corresponding to the upper end of the Gamow window at T ≃ 1.8 GK. Therefore, above T = 1.8 GK the NON-SMOKER Hauser-Feshbach reaction rates [213], normalized to the calculation result Appendix 36 Cl(p,α) 33

div.head: S
div.p: The rate of the positive Q-value reaction 36 Cl(p,α) 33 S is evaluated considering experimental data for its reverse reaction 33 S(α,p) 36 Cl [145]. The data cover the 0.8-1.5 MeV/nucleon energy range and were obtained from direct inverse kinematics measurements performed at the University of Notre Dame using an activation technique. The 36 Cl(p,α) 33 S cross sections are obtained from the experimental 33 S(α,p) 36 Cl cross sections using the detailed balance theorem, taking only the ground state contribution into account. TALYS calculations are then used to reproduce the deduced 36 Cl(p,α) 33 S ground state cross section by adjusting the parameters of proton and α optical model potentials. The resulting optical model potentials are then used to compute the astrophysical reaction rate of 36 Cl(p,α) 33 S, and the results are given in Table 10.

The 35 K(p,γ) 36 Ca reaction rate is dominated by resonant capture through the first excited 2 + state in 36 Ca up to a temperature of around 2 GK. [127] performed a 37 Ca(p,d) 36 Ca measurement at GANIL using the CRYPTA liquid hydrogen target and determined the proton branching of this state, corresponding to a resonance at 445 keV. Combining the measurement with a shell model calculation for the γ-partial width results in an updated resonance strength. The numerical results, including the stellar enhancement factor, are given in Table 10.

We follow the evaluation of Hou et al. [247], who determined the reaction rate from the 8 lowest energy resonances that can be populated by l = 0, 1, 2, 3 orbital angular momentum proton capture. Resonance energies are determined from the known excitation energies in the 43 Ca mirror nucleus and an updated Q-value based on a recent mass measurement at the HIRFL cooler storage ring in Lanzhou [273]. No Coulomb shifts are applied, instead, a 100 keV uncertainty is assumed. Proton spectroscopic factors are taken from experimental (p,d) stripping reaction data for 43 Ca when available, otherwise the shell model is used. γ-widths were determined from the known level lifetimes in 43 Ca. A direct capture component is included as well. A SEF is applied but only impacts the rate above 4 GK. The numerical astrophysical reaction rates of 42 Ti(p,γ) 43 V are given in Table 10.

Appendix 55 Ni(p,γ) 56 Cu Excitation energies of the three lowest energy resonances were determined from inbeam γ-ray spectroscopy using a radioactive 56 Ni beam impinging on a CD 2 target at the NSCL at Michigan State University [96]. Resonance states in 56 Cu were populated via the (d,2n) reaction channel. Experimental data on excitation energies were complemented with GXPF1A shell model calculations for γ-and proton-widths, as well as for resonance properties of higher lying resonances. More recently, the mass of 56 Cu was measured precisely using the LEBIT Penning trap at Michigan State University leading to a revised reaction Q-value of 582(6) keV, and consequently revised resonance energies, and, using updated shell model calculations, revised proton and γ-widths [153]. We adopt the recommended rate from [153], corrected with a SEF, and tabulated in Table 11.
Appendix 56 Ni(p,γ) 57

div.head: Cu
div.p: For T ≤ 2 GK, the reaction rate is dominated by 4 resonances at E r = 338, 418, 1708 and 1835 keV corresponding to the well known 4 first excited states in 57 Cu. Recently, proton spectroscopic factors have been determined experimentally using angle integrated cross sections for (d,n) and (d,p) (populating the isospin mirror) transfer reactions in inverse kinematics performed at NSCL at Michigan State University [111]. These are important for the two lowest lying resonances, where resonance strengths are dominated by proton widths. We adopt the resonance parameters from [111], which combine experimental data and shell model calculations to determine γ-widths. For T > 2 GK, the slope of the NON-SMOKER [213] Hauser-Feshbach reaction rate is used, normalized to the rate at T = 2 GK. A SEF is applied to correct the highest temperature data points above 5 GK. Results are given in Table 11.
Appendix 57 Cu(p,γ) 58

div.head: Zn
div.p: We adopt direct capture S-factors and resonance properties from [112], who determined excitation energies of the 6 lowest lying resonant states in 58 Zn using γ-ray spectroscopy using the d( 57 Cu, 58 Zn)n reaction in inverse kinematics at NSCL at Michigan State University. Resonance energies were determined using the Q-value of 2279 (50) keV given in [112], which agrees within uncertainties with the AME20 Q-value (2270(50)) [157]. Spectroscopic factors, proton-and γ-widths are calculated using fp-shell model calculations with the GXPF1A interaction. The calculated reaction rate, corrected with a SEF, are tabulated in Table 11.

Appendix 65 As(p,γ) 66 Se [274] reports a shell model calculation of the 65 As(p,γ) 66 Se reaction rate below T = 2 GK, which is adopted in the present evaluation. Capture on the ground state and the thermally excited first excited state in 65 As is included. This calculation follows
